g.data-narration: Welcome to Memory Operations. Today we explore how the processor reads and writes data to memory, the foundation of all computing.
Computer memory stores programs and data. The processor must read (load) and write (store) data to execute any program.
g.text: The primary function of computer memory is to store programs and data.
The processor performs two fundamental operations:
These form the basis of all memory interactions.

g.data-narration: We'll learn the step-by-step Read and Write processes, Register Transfer Notation, memory access time, and word alignment.
Read operation transfers data from memory to the processor. The processor places an address, activates Read signal, and receives data.
g.text: A Read operation transfers data FROM memory TO the processor.
Processor places address on address bus
Memory locates data and places on data bus

g.data-narration: The Read operation uses MAR to hold the address, MDR to receive data, with address bus, data bus, and control signals coordinating the transfer.
Write operation transfers data from processor to memory. The processor sends address, data, and write signal to store the value.
g.text: A Write operation transfers data FROM the processor TO memory.
Processor places target address on address bus
Processor places data to write on data bus

g.data-narration: During Write, MAR holds the target address, MDR holds the data to write. Memory stores the MDR contents at the MAR address.
Register Transfer Notation describes memory operations formally. R ← [LOC] means read memory into register. [LOC] ← R means write register to memory.
g.text: Square brackets [ ] denote "contents of memory location"
Load: Copy memory location LOC contents into register R1
Store: Copy register R1 contents into memory location LOC

g.data-narration: Memory access time is critical for performance. DRAM takes 50-100 nanoseconds, while SRAM is faster at 5-10 nanoseconds.
g.text: Time between initiation and completion of Read/Write operations

g.data-narration: Memory is organized in a hierarchy: fast small registers and cache, slower larger RAM, and even slower secondary storage like SSDs.
Word-aligned memory access is efficient. A 4-byte word should start at addresses 0, 4, 8 for aligned access. Unaligned access requires multiple operations.
g.text: Multi-byte data should be accessed at aligned addresses for efficiency

g.data-narration: During instruction execution, memory is accessed for instruction fetch, operand fetch, and result storage. Example: ADD R1, [1000] involves three steps.
g.text: Memory is accessed multiple times during instruction execution:
→ Fetch instruction → Read [1000] → Add to R1

g.data-narration: Remember: Read transfers memory to processor, Write transfers processor to memory. Use MAR, MDR, address bus, data bus, and control signals.

g.text: Two fundamental operations: Read (Load) and Write (Store)
Read: Memory → Processor | Write: Processor → Memory
Uses MAR (address), MDR (data), address bus, data bus, control signals
RTN: R ← [LOC] for Load, [LOC] ← R for Store

g.data-narration: Thank you for learning Memory Operations! These concepts are essential for understanding computer architecture, systems programming, and performance optimization.
g.text: Foundation for cache, DMA, and memory hierarchy optimization
